The component failed to render properly, likely due to a configuration issue in Storybook. Here are some common causes and how you can address them:

  1. Missing Context/Providers: You can use decorators to supply specific contexts or providers, which are sometimes necessary for components to render correctly. For detailed instructions on using decorators, please visit the Decorators documentation.
  2. Misconfigured Webpack or Vite: Verify that Storybook picks up all necessary settings for loaders, plugins, and other relevant parameters. You can find step-by-step guides for configuring Webpack or Vite with Storybook.
  3. Missing Environment Variables: Your Storybook may require specific environment variables to function as intended. You can set up custom environment variables as outlined in the Environment Variables documentation.

  • A design system for the State of Maryland

Maryland Web Design System (MDWDS)

A design system for the State of Maryland

The Maryland Web Design System (MDWDS) provides design, code, and guidance to help Maryland government teams design, build, and measure high-performing digital services that are consistent, accessible, well-designed, and mobile-friendly.

The MDWDS is currently under development and still in its early stages.

The MDWDS will evolve over time in response to the needs of users, as well as to changing technology. As new UI components and patterns are released, we will announce them in the MDWDS Contributors Google Chat channel.

Playwright Testing

The Maryland Web Design System uses Playwright for end-to-end visual regression testing. This ensures components render consistently across different viewports and browsers.

Overview

The Playwright test suite:

  • Tests components via Storybook stories
  • Captures visual snapshots across multiple viewport sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop)
  • Runs in Docker containers for cross-platform consistency
  • Includes accessibility testing with @axe-core/playwright [DOCS VISIBLE TEXT] TABLE OF CONTENTS Overview Running Tests Run all tests Run specific tests Update snapshots Project Structure Writing Tests Basic Example Custom Storybook Fixture Screenshot Options Playwright Testing The Maryland Web Design System uses Playwright for end-to-end visual regression testing. This ensures components render consistently across different viewports and browsers. Overview The Playwright test suite: Tests components via Storybook stories Captures visual snapshots across multiple viewport sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop) Runs in Docker containers for cross-platform consistency Includes accessibility testing with @axe-core/playwright Uses custom fixtures for streamlined Storybook integration Running Tests Tests are executed via a bash script that runs Playwright in a Docker container, ensuring consistent results across different development environments. Important: Prior to running tests, a static storybook build must exist. Run pnpm build to compile the static assets, and pnpm storybook:build to create a static storybook build. After a test run has completed, a report is generated. For local runs, this exists in /playwright-report. For CI runs, this is a downloadable .zip file available on the github actions summary page. To view the report, open the enclosed index.html page in a browser. Run all tests pnpm test:e2e Copy Run specific tests Use the --tests flag with a pattern to match test file names: pnpm test:e2e --tests accordion pnpm test:e2e --tests hero Copy Update snapshots When component visuals change intentionally, update snapshots: # Update all snapshots pnpm test:e2e:update-snapshots # Update specific component snapshots pnpm test:e2e:update-snapshots --tests accordion Copy Project Structure e2e/ ├── playwright.config.ts # Playwright configuration ├── src/ │ ├── fixtures/ │ │ └── test.ts # Custom Storybook fixtures │ ├── helpers/ │ │ ├── storybook.ts # Storybook navigation helpers │ │ ├── images.ts

Breadcrumb

Overview

The MDWDS Breadcrumb component helps users navigate back to previous pages. It's based on USWDS Breadcrumb with Maryland-specific styling.

Features:

  • Light and dark color variants
  • Support for wrapping and RDFa metadata
  • Accessible with ARIA attributes
  • Current page is not linked (rendered as plain text)

Usage:

  • Pass an array of page strings or objects with label and href properties
  • The last item in the array is automatically marked as the current page (not linked)

Hero

Overview

The Landing Main Hero component displays a full-width landscape background image with text overlay for landing pages.

Features:

  • Full-width background image (gradient should be baked into the image)
  • Background image positioning for scenic display
  • Title with auto-detected eyebrow (titles starting with "Welcome to " display the prefix as styled eyebrow text)
  • Description (optional)

This component is ideal for:

  • Maryland.gov homepage

Important: The background image should be exported from Figma with both the Photo and Rectangle gradient layers merged together. The component does not apply a gradient overlay via CSS.

Use the Image control to switch between different Maryland landscape photos.

Links

Overview

Maryland Design System links provide enhanced styling and accessibility features for various link types. Use the variant control to switch between link types.

  • Default Links: Standard links with Maryland styling.
  • Document Links: Links for downloadable files with file type and size.
  • Document Heading Links: Document links within heading elements for listings and cards. Includes file type and size.
  • Document Card Links: Document links in card context without hover underline. Includes file type and size.
  • Labelled Links: Links with descriptive labels above them for additional context.
  • External Links: Links that navigate away from the current site. Includes icon. Toggle the "external" control on any variant.
  • Skip Links: Accessibility links that allow keyboard users to bypass navigation. Includes "skipTo" control for "main" and "sidebar" options.

Statewide Banner

What is it?

The Banner tells people that the site (or application) is a secure and official Maryland government site. The Banner displays key information without being visually distracting

When to use it?

It is required on all pages of a website or application. It is positioned at the top of all pages.

Recommended Usage (via CDN)

For most projects, we recommend using the CDN-hosted version of MDWDS.

Using the CDN means:

  • You’ll always get the latest branding, accessibility, and analytics updates.
  • No need to redeploy your app when the design system changes.

Statewide Footer

The maryland-statewide-footer component provides a consistent, accessible footer across Maryland.gov websites and applications. It reinforces public trust, improves navigation, and aligns with Maryland’s digital service standards and the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS).

✅ Recommended Usage (via CDN)

To ensure you always receive the latest version with official branding and compliance updates, use the CDN-hosted version:



Copy

Using the CDN ensures downstream applications always stay up to date with the latest layout, links, and accessibility improvements — no manual deployments required.

Responsive layout with mobile-first design

Maryland state services
Government portals
Privacy policies
Emergency alerts and notifications

Customization Guidance

This component is intended to be non-editable in most use cases. If you need to override layout or behavior (e.g., embed it inside other components), MDDS recommends doing so only through scoped CSS or by submitting a feature request.

The Maryland Statewide Footer is a standardized footer component that appears across all state digital services. It includes grouped links to top services, government portals, policies, and emergency alerts.

It is fully responsive, styled using MDWDS, and requires no configuration or data bindings.

Banner

Banners identify official websites of government organizations in the United States. They also help visitors understand whether a website is official and secure.

When to use

  • You should use the banner to identify your site as an official site of the State of Maryland.
  • The banner explains how to identify an official .gov domain and that these sites have secure HTTPS connections. Using the banner component is the best way to assure visitors that they’re connected to an official site.

When to use something else

  • Do NOT use the banner on non-government domains such as a .com or .org.

Collection

Overview

The USWDS Collection component displays a compact list of multiple related items like articles or events that link to original sources.

This generator allows you to:

  • Display a list of items with headings, descriptions, and metadata.
  • Include optional images or icons for each item.
  • Show date information in standard or calendar format.
  • Add tags to categorize items.
  • Choose between default and condensed variants.
  • Optionally enable Google Analytics tracking attributes.

Combo Box

Overview

The USWDS Combo Box is an enhanced select dropdown that combines a text input with a filterable option list. It provides type-ahead functionality, making it ideal for long lists of options.

This component provides:

  • Type-ahead filtering as users type
  • Keyboard navigation (arrow keys, enter, escape)
  • Support for large option lists
  • Clear button to reset selection
  • Required field support
  • Disabled state
  • Default selected value
  • Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Full WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility compliance

Note: The combo box uses USWDS JavaScript for keyboard navigation and filtering functionality. This is automatically loaded when the component renders.

Date Picker

Overview

The USWDS Date Picker component provides an accessible calendar popup widget for date selection, powered by USWDS JavaScript.

This generator allows you to:

  • Display an interactive calendar popup
  • Constrain selection with minimum and maximum dates
  • Set default values
  • Support keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Page Up/Down, Home/End)
  • Provide range date highlighting
  • Configure Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Follow WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements

Note: This component requires USWDS JavaScript to be loaded. The calendar widget initializes automatically on page load.

Date Range Picker

Overview

The USWDS Date Range Picker component allows users to select a date range using two connected date picker components (start date and end date).

This generator allows you to:

  • Display two synchronized date picker widgets
  • Constrain the overall range with minimum and maximum dates
  • Validate that the end date is after the start date
  • Support keyboard navigation across both pickers
  • Configure Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Follow WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements

Note: This component requires USWDS JavaScript to be loaded. It internally uses two Date Picker components that work together to enforce valid date ranges.

File Input

Overview

The USWDS File Input component provides an accessible and user-friendly way to upload files.

  • Display error and disabled states
  • Provide hint text for file requirements
  • Enable drag-and-drop functionality (via USWDS JS)
  • Optionally enable Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Configure accessibility attributes (ARIA)

Form

Overview

The USWDS Form component provides a consistent container and styling for form elements.

This generator allows you to:

  • Create form containers with proper semantic markup
  • Use form groups to organize related fields
  • Implement fieldset and legend for grouped inputs
  • Apply large variant styling (usa-form--large)
  • Display complete form examples with various field types
  • Optionally enable Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Configure accessibility attributes (ARIA)

Input Mask

Overview

The USWDS Input Mask component provides formatted input fields for common data patterns like phone numbers, SSN, and ZIP codes.

This generator allows you to:

  • Apply predefined masks (phone, SSN, ZIP code)
  • Display hint text showing expected format
  • Automatically format input as user types
  • Validate input format
  • Optionally enable Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Configure accessibility attributes (ARIA)

Note: This component requires USWDS JavaScript to be loaded for live masking functionality.

Language Selector

Overview

The USWDS Language Selector provides a dropdown interface for users to select content in different languages. It supports both dropdown (for headers) and menu variants (for footers).

This component provides:

  • Dropdown variant for use in site headers
  • Small variant modifier for compact displays
  • Language list with native names (e.g., "Español", "中文", "العربية")
  • Proper lang attributes on each option for accessibility
  • Keyboard navigation with USWDS JavaScript
  • Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Full WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility compliance

Note: The language selector uses USWDS JavaScript for accordion functionality. This is automatically loaded when the component renders.

Overview

  • Include optional required field marking
  • Configure Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Follow WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements

Note: This component uses simple text inputs rather than a calendar widget. For calendar-based selection, use the Date Picker component.

      The Importance of Web Accessibility

      Web accessibility ensures that people with disabilities can perceive, understand, navigate, and interact with websites and tools. In Maryland, WCAG 2.1 AA compliance is legally mandated for all state websites.

      Key Principles

      • Perceivable: Information must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive
      • Operable: Interface components must be operable by all users
      • Understandable: Information and operation must be understandable

Site Alert

Overview

The USWDS Site Alert component provides a consistent way to inform users of critical site-wide information.

Variants reflect the importance and urgency of the alert:

  • info (default): General messages and updates.
  • warning: Important non-emergency warnings.
  • emergency: Emergency alerts requiring immediate attention.
  • success: Confirmation or success notifications.

This component:

  • Supports full accessibility customization, including ARIA roles and live regions.

Time Picker

Overview

The USWDS Time Picker component provides an accessible dropdown time selection widget with filtering capabilities, powered by USWDS JavaScript.

This generator allows you to:

  • Display a filterable time dropdown with customizable intervals
  • Constrain selection with minimum and maximum times
  • Configure time step intervals (e.g., 15, 30, 60 minutes)
  • Support keyboard filtering by typing
  • Set default time values
  • Configure Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Follow WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements

Note: This component requires USWDS JavaScript to be loaded. The time picker initializes automatically on page load with combobox-like functionality.

Overview

The USWDS Validation component provides inline validation feedback for form fields with error and success states.

This generator allows you to:

  • Display error messages below invalid fields
  • Show success states for validated fields
  • Mark required fields with asterisk indicators
  • Provide accessible error announcements
  • Style inputs with error/success states
  • Optionally enable Google Analytics tracking attributes
  • Configure accessibility attributes (ARIA)

Note: This component requires USWDS JavaScript to be loaded for dynamic validation functionality.

Adopt Conventional Comments for Code Reviews

Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-06-18

🚀 Context

As our team scales and collaborates more frequently on code changes, it’s essential to ensure that code reviews are clear, respectful, and actionable. Inconsistent review comments can lead to confusion or miscommunication, slowing down the review process and frustrating team members.

To address this, we are adopting Conventional Comments. This structured framework for giving feedback promotes consistency, helps categorize comments (e.g., suggestions vs. blocking issues), and fosters a more collaborative review culture.

Adopt Conventional Commits for Versioning and Clarity

Status: Proposed
Date: 2025-06-01

🚀 Context

Maintaining clear and meaningful commit history is critical to long-term project sustainability. Inconsistent or unclear commit messages make it difficult to:

  • Understand the history of a codebase
  • Automate versioning (e.g., semantic release)
  • Generate changelogs
  • Improve collaboration across contributors

Conventional Commits is a lightweight convention that standardizes commit message formats for better readability and automation. It plays well with semantic versioning (SemVer) and changelog generation tools, and integrates into many modern CI/CD workflows.

[optional scope]: [optional body] [optional footer(s)] Copy Example feat(auth): add login redirect after signup fix(forms): handle null values in required fields docs(readme): update contributing guidelines chore(deps): bump astro from 3.5 to 3.6 Copy Supported Commit Types Type Purpose feat A new feature fix A bug fix docs Documentation changes only style Formatting, whitespace, etc. (no code changes) refactor Code change that neither fixes nor adds a feature test Adding or fixing tests chore Maintenance tasks (e.g., bumping deps) ci CI/CD configuration or scripts perf Performance improvements build Changes that affect the build system or deps 🎉 Benefits ✅ Enables semantic versioning and automatic changelog generation ✅ Provides consistent, meaningful history across all projects ✅ Makes it easier for new contributors to understand changes ✅ Supports tools like semantic-release, release-please, and others ⚖️ Consequences Requires a small learning curve and upfront discipline from contributors. Choose Web Component Framework

Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-06-17

🚀 Context

We need a consistent and scalable way to build web components for use across multiple applications. While it's possible to build web components with plain JavaScript using the Web Components standard (Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, etc.), doing so without a framework increases the likelihood of repeated boilerplate code, reduced maintainability, and lack of built-in ergonomics for templating, reactivity, and bundling.

Our goal is to empower developers to build fast, reusable, accessible components with minimal overhead, while maintaining flexibility and performance. These components need to work across multiple environments, including CMS platforms, static sites, and micro frontends—which often use a mix of frameworks or no framework at all.

Exclude USWDS Styles from Core Web Components

Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-06-18

🚀 Context

As we develop MDWDS using Web Components, we've been evaluating how to incorporate the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS) into our component library. USWDS offers a wide range of design tokens, components, and patterns that align with federal digital design standards, but its Sass-based architecture introduces technical challenges when used directly inside encapsulated Shadow DOM components.

We noticed that including USWDS component packages in core web components leads to unintended side effects like duplicated style rules, stylesheet bloat, and maintenance risk due to tightly coupled dependencies. Additionally, supporting customizable USWDS theming across components embedded in the Shadow DOM introduces complexity in theme propagation and architectural consistency.

Architecture Decision Records

🚀 What is an ADR?

An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) captures a single, important decision about the architecture of a software system — along with its context and consequences.

It’s a lightweight and version-controlled way to document why decisions were made, so future team members (including future-you!) can understand the rationale behind them.

🫡 Why We Use ADRs

  • Transparency: Everyone understands the "why" behind technical decisions.

Use maryland- Prefix for Web Components and CSS Variables

Status: Accepted
Date: 2025-01-01

🚀 Context

We needed a prefix to prevent collisions with other web components, design systems, or CSS variables in consuming applications. Prefixing is a best practice for design systems, particularly in government or enterprise contexts where multiple frameworks and libraries may coexist.

In late 2024, we polled MDWDS community members on their preferences. The majority strongly supported the use of the maryland- prefix over alternatives like md or toolkit.

. This aligns with the Web Components Naming Guidelines and supports our long-term goal of namespace clarity. ⚖️ Consequences Pros: Reduces risk of name collisions with other libraries (e.g., uswds, bootstrap, material). Improves clarity and recognizability across teams and projects. Future-proofs the system for broader use across state agencies. Cons: Slightly longer component and variable names (e.g., maryland-button vs md-button). Existing adopters may need to refactor if previously using another convention. 👨🏾‍💻 Alternatives Considered md-: Brief, but often interpreted as “Markdown” or “Medical Doctor.” Too ambiguous for our use case. toolkit-: Clearer than md- but didn’t resonate with users. Community feedback suggested it felt too technical and awkward in usage.

When to Use Web Components vs. Standard HTML + CSS in MDWDS

Status: Proposed
Date: 2025-06-01

🚀 Context

Web Components offer encapsulation, reusability, and interoperability across frameworks, making them an appealing choice for building a design system. However, using them in every scenario introduces unnecessary complexity—especially when standard HTML is already expressive, broadly supported, and accessible.

📘 How-To Guides

Welcome to the MDWDS How-To Guides folder. This section contains practical, step-by-step instructions for common tasks, workflows, and integration points with the Maryland Web Design System (MDWDS).

These guides are designed to help both new and experienced developers get things done quickly — with clear instructions and minimal guesswork.

🫡 Why We Use How-To Guides

How-To guides are different from reference docs or design decisions. They're focused on doing, not deciding.

  • ✅ Task-oriented: Solve a specific problem or accomplish a goal.
  • 🧭 Prescriptive: Tell you exactly what to do — and what to avoid.
  • 👥 Collaborative: Help teammates (and future-you!) move faster with consistent patterns.
  • 🧰 Reusable: Can be linked from internal docs, wikis, onboarding checklists, and Storybook.
